Pico della Mirandola’s Commento sopra una canzone d’amore (ca. 1486) is one of the most dazzling and difficult works of early Renaissance Platonism. It was written just after the scandal of Pico’s attempted elopement with Margherita of Arezzo, and before his Conclusiones and Oratio . The Commento is his first systematic philosophical text — and, as Michael J. B. Allen notes, “his first laboratory of metaphysics,” written in the form of a commentary on his friend Girolamo Benivieni’s Canzone d’amore 【154:17†M. V. Dougherty – Pico della Mirandola†L1-L17】. I. Background and Form Pico’s Commento was composed in 1486, at his villa in Fratta near Perugia, while preparing for his grand disputation in Rome. It takes Benivieni’s poem Amor, dalle cui man sospeso è ’l freno / del mio cor pende as its basis. The poem itself summarizes themes from Ficino’s De amore , but Pico uses it as a springboard to build his own philosophical theology of love and intellect【154:17†M. V. Dougherty – Pico della Mirandola†L5-L15】. The Commento originally existed in three books. Later, Benivieni suppressed references to Ficino and reorganized the text, producing a more devotional, expurgated version in 1500. The complete, unexpurgated version — discovered and edited by Eugenio Garin in 1942 — restores Pico’s combative stance toward Ficino’s interpretation of Plato and gives a truer sense of its intellectual daring【154:0†M. V. Dougherty – Pico della Mirandola†L39-L50】. II. Central Themes 1. Poetic Theology (Theologia Poetica) Pico’s Commento is less about love psychology than about poetic theology : the notion that ancient myths and poems conceal philosophical truths. As he writes: “The ancient theologians, who under poetic veils hid their mysteries, taught divine truths in the form of fables” ( Commento , I.3). Allen describes this as Pico’s commitment to poetic metaphysics : interpreting the myths of Venus, Cupid, and Apollo as allegories of divine intellect and love, “a gentile scripture revealed through inspired poets”【154:16†M. V. Dougherty – Pico della Mirandola†L31-L41】. Busi and Ebgi emphasize that unlike Landino, Pico does not Christianize the pagan myths but treats them as hieroglyphs of Platonic wisdom: “The ancient theologians... are seen as a mine of Platonic teaching, and Pico works finely to transform Venuses, Uranias, and Nymphs into degrees of the immaterial world.”【154:5†(I millenni) Giulio Busi & Raphael Ebgi†L19-L27】 2. Love as Ascent to the Angelic Mind The love described by Benivieni becomes, for Pico, a metaphysical ladder: The lower Venus corresponds to sensual love, rooted in imagination. The celestial Venus symbolizes the angelic intellect, where love and knowledge coincide. “Through love the soul unites its partial intellect to the universal intellect or angelic mind, the first of creatures and the lodging of ideal beauty.” (Allen’s paraphrase of Commento , bk. II)【154:2†Allen – Studies in the Platonism of Marsilio Ficino and Giovanni Pico†L33-L38】. In this ascent, eros becomes a function of energeia : the human mind actualizing its divine likeness by moving from potential to act — an early expression of the anthropology Pico will elaborate in the Oratio . 3. Polemic with Ficino The Commento is also Pico’s first break with Ficino. Akopyan notes that Pico “misses few opportunities to clarify his position on astrology and magic,” attacking Ficino’s “misunderstanding of what constituted the Neoplatonic tradition.” He accuses Ficino of confusing the “First Intellect” — the divine Wisdom of Hermes and Zoroaster — with the Christian Logos: “The ancient theologians called the First Creation ‘the Son of God, the Wisdom, the Mind of God,’ which had nothing in common with the Christian Jesus.”【154:1†Brill – Debating the Stars in the Italian Renaissance†L17-L31】 This move reflects Pico’s attempt to re-ground Neoplatonism in ancient theology rather than in Ficino’s Christocentric reading. 4. The Unity of Love and Being Pico’s treatment of love anticipates his metaphysics of De ente et uno . Love is the dynamic expression of divine unity — “that by which the soul returns to the One.” The myth of Venus’s birth from the foam of the sea is read as the emergence of intelligible beauty from the divine abyss. Allen observes that for Pico, “beauty is the radiance of unity perceived in multiplicity” — thus eros becomes an intellectual act, a motion of the mind toward simplicity【154:6†Allen – Studies in the Platonism of Marsilio Ficino and Giovanni Pico†L33-L35】. III. Style and Tone Pico’s prose is alternately lyrical and scholastic. Busi notes that even though the Commento was written after the Margherita scandal, it still “glitters with Platonic sensuality” — “a lucente lascivia, Platonic though provocative.” Its language fuses scholastic precision with erotic luminosity, echoing the tension between the philosopher and the lover【154:4†(I millenni) Giulio Busi & Raphael Ebgi†L27-L35】. IV. Key Quotations (in paraphrase and partial translation) “Amore è figlio di Penia e Poros” — Love is born of poverty and resource, signifying the soul’s yearning toward divine plenitude through lack and intellect ( Commento , I.5). “Gli antichi teologi... sotto velamento poetico coprirono i loro misteri” — The poets are the first theologians, veiling divine mysteries in myth ( Commento , I.3). “Venere celeste è l’intelletto angelico... nel quale l’anima trova la vera bellezza” — Celestial Venus symbolizes the angelic intellect where beauty dwells. “Apollo è invocato per la eloquenza del vero” — Apollo is not a pagan god but the light of intellectual eloquence, the gift of expressing truth beautifully【154:14†(I millenni) Giulio Busi & Raphael Ebgi†L35-L43】. V. Philosophical and Historical Importance The Commento stands at the crossroads of Pico’s intellectual life: It develops the idea of poetic revelation that grounds his later Hermeticism. It anticipates the angelic anthropology of the Oratio and the Heptaplus . It inaugurates the first Ficino–Pico controversy , foreshadowing the debates on creation, astrology, and divine mediation that dominate his Disputationes adversus astrologiam divinatricem . As Allen concludes: “The Commento has a rapid-fire brilliance… not a love treatise but an experiment in metaphysical poetics, where myth, theology, and intellect converge”【154:7†M. V. Dougherty – Pico della Mirandola†L28-L36】. In short: the Commento sopra una canzone d’amore is Pico’s first statement of his philosophical theology — a fusion of Platonic eros, Aristotelian act, and Hermetic revelation — in which poetic beauty becomes the language of metaphysical ascent.

Girolamo Benivieni (1453–1542) was one of the most versatile and influential figures in late-Quattrocento Florence — a poet, musician, translator, and disciple of both Pico della Mirandola and Girolamo Savonarola. His long career can be divided into three overlapping phases: (1) a Laurentian poet and musician; (2) Pico’s collaborator and philosophical interpreter; and (3) a Savonarolan reformer and civic prophet. 1. Early Humanist Poet in Lorenzo’s Circle Benivieni was a prodigy of the Medicean academy. Sherry Roush notes that by his teens he “delighted the company with his ability to recite poems composed spontaneously and played the viol, earning for himself the nickname of the ‘Other Orpheus’” . His early works were Petrarchan love lyrics and eclogues modeled on Poliziano, Lorenzo, and Virgil, marked by “bucolic sweetness and Platonic allegory.” He was well-versed in Greek and Hebrew and was invited to translate the Bible into the vernacular late in life【165†Two Poems†L43-L50】. 2. Collaboration with Giovanni Pico della Mirandola Olga Zorzi Pugliese shows that Pico and Benivieni formed an exceptional intellectual and emotional friendship. They met in 1479, and together participated in the Platonic Academy and charitable projects at San Marco【166†Pugliese†L347-L353】. Pico even entrusted Benivieni with distributing his alms to the poor and referred to him as acuto vir ingenio et perspicaci (“a man of sharp and penetrating intellect”). The Canzone d’Amore and Pico’s Commentary: Benivieni’s Canzone dell’amor celeste e divino inspired Pico’s celebrated Commento sopra una canzone d’amore (1486). The poem allegorizes divine beauty and the soul’s ascent; Pico’s commentary turns it into a full metaphysical treatise on love and intellect. Mutual Influence: Benivieni later confessed that Pico’s Commento and the Oratio de hominis dignitate inspired him to rewrite his own early love poetry as sacred allegory. In his 1500 Commento sopra a più sue canzone et sonetti dello amore et della bellezza divina , he calls Pico “ unico veramente et singulare ornamento de la nostra età ” and laments his “too-certain and bitter death” as a calamity for Christendom【166†Pugliese†L347-L351】. Hebrew and Cabalistic Learning: Pugliese documents that Benivieni learned elements of Kabbalah from Pico and even compiled a Latin–Hebrew lexicon. Pico wrote of their shared “ nostra de numeris doctrina ,” hinting at their joint study of number mysticism【166†Pugliese†L349-L350】. 3. Savonarolan Conversion and Civic Prophecy By the 1490s Benivieni underwent a dramatic spiritual conversion under Savonarola’s influence. He repudiated his earlier courtly poetry as “too worldly” and became “the official translator of Savonarola’s texts,” rendering De simplicite Christianae vitae into Italian and the Epistola de humiltà into Latin【165†Two Poems†L47-L52】. His Savonarolan poetry, such as the Canzone on the Bliss Promised to Florence (1496) and the Canzone for the Bonfire of Vanities (1497), proclaims Florence the nova Hierusalem —a city chosen for divine renewal: “O city more fortunate than all others… you are built on sacred mountains and endowed with holy doors… Arise, O new Jerusalem, and look; behold your glory.”【165†Two Poems†L271-L274】 Even after Savonarola’s execution, he modified his verse “to emphasize the prophet’s saintliness.” His later life was devoted to publishing devotional poetry and defending Savonarola’s legacy. 4. The Dante Editor and Piagnone Patriot Benivieni’s Cantico in laude di Dante (1506) served as the proem to the Florentine edition of the Divine Comedy . Roush shows that Benivieni frames Dante as a prophetic spokesman for the Piagnoni (Savonarola’s followers) and for a republican Florence purified of Medicean corruption. The poem, a 199-line terza rima vision, recounts a dream encounter with Dante’s spirit, who endorses Savonarola’s moral reforms: “By putting his vision of Florentine civic direction in Dante’s mouth, Benivieni appropriates Dante’s authority to promote what… was a risky ideological position.” In the poem’s closing prophecy Dante hails a “New Florence,” and Benivieni implicitly identifies himself as Dante’s poetic heir—the Florentine Orpheus reborn in the age of repentance. 5. The Benivieni Brothers and the Savonarolan Defense Uberto Motta’s review of Domenico Benivieni’s Trattato in difesa di Girolamo Savonarola (1496–97) clarifies the family’s collective role in the Piagnone movement. Domenico—Girolamo’s brother—was a Dominican scholar who composed scholastic defenses of Savonarola, stressing “the conformity of Savonarola’s doctrine with Holy Scripture and the analogy between his earthly fate and that of Christ”【164†Aevum Review†L905-L907】. Girolamo echoed these themes poetically in his civic hymns and commentaries. 6. Later Years and Legacy Benivieni lived to 89, tirelessly revising his works and defending Pico and Savonarola. In 1515 he wrote that he was “ uno armario di tutti e suoi secreti ” — “a repository of all [Pico’s] secrets” — and requested burial beside him. He was interred in San Marco with the epitaph: “ Ne disiunctus post mortem locus ossa separet quorum animos in vita coniunxit amor ” — “May no place after death divide the bones of those whose souls were joined by love in life.”【166†Pugliese†L350-L351】 7. Significance Benivieni’s life traces the arc of Florentine spirituality from Platonic love to Christian reform . With Pico , he translated Neoplatonism into vernacular theology, uniting poetic eros with intellect. With Savonarola , he turned that vision toward civic repentance and moral renewal. As Dante’s editor , he fused both strands into a prophetic nationalism. Modern scholars—Roush, Pugliese, and Motta—view him as a crucial bridge between Ficinian Platonism, Pichian syncretism, and Savonarolan apocalypticism: a poet who “spiritualized humanism without renouncing its eloquence.”

Angels, in this schema, are intellectual forms whose being and motion manifest God’s energeia within the limits of creation. “The angelic intellect is not a separate demiurgic agency, nor an astral pneuma, but the purest created form in which divine act ( actus Dei ) is received without distortion. It demonstrates how form and matter , act and potency , articulate one another within the divine order.” — Howlett, Re-Evaluating Pico (Palgrave 2019), p. 142 Pico himself, in Heptaplus I.ii–iii, writes: “The Spirit of the Lord was borne upon the waters, that is, upon the angelic intellect … God said, ‘Let there be light,’ and light was made in the angel, the light of intelligible forms.” Howlett comments that this moment “transposes miracle into ontology” —the angelic mind becomes the metaphysical form of divine operation rather than a pneumatic agent that produces marvels. Where Ficino had seen a chain of “spiritus” linking heaven and earth, Pico replaces the chain with intelligible causality : angels as form in act . “For Pico, to understand angels is to understand how the world’s form is informed by divine act. The miraculous becomes the intelligible structure of being itself.” — Howlett, p. 143 Thus, angels are not magicians or intermediaries but instances of created act —living demonstrations of how divine energeia works through Aristotelian form–matter relations. 🜍 2. Amos Edelheit — Intellect as Participation in Divine Act Topic Sentence: Edelheit emphasizes Pico’s use of Aquinas and Dionysius to define intellect as participation in divine act , making angels the model of pure energeia rather than intermediary gods. Explanation: In Ficino, Pico and Savonarola: The Evolution of Humanist Theology (1461–1498) (Brill 2008), Edelheit shows that Pico synthesizes Thomist metaphysics (act/potency) and Dionysian theology (procession and return). The result is that angels, as intellects fully actualized, become created analogues of divine act , not independent powers between God and man. “The angelic intellect is not a lesser divinity or cosmic daemon, but the first created participation in divine energeia . Pico adopts Aquinas’s definition of the angel as forma subsistens and couples it with Dionysius’s view of angelic orders as ‘living operations’ ( energeiai zōsai ).” — Edelheit, p. 221 In the Heptaplus , Pico echoes both sources: “The angels are composed of act and potency, although there is dispute as to what is act and what potency.” Edelheit notes that Pico is adapting Summa Theologiae I.54.2, where Aquinas calls angelic knowledge “a single act proceeding from their essence.” But Pico’s reading is more dynamic: “Through Dionysius, Pico recovers the notion of energeia as divine communication: the angels, being pure intellect, exist as ongoing acts of contemplation. They are not mediators between two substances, but continuous operations of the first cause.” — Edelheit, p. 223 This synthesis transforms Scholastic angelology into metaphysical anthropology : the human mind, by knowing and loving, participates in the same act that constitutes the angels’ being. 🜍 3. Giulio Busi & Raphael Ebgi — Angelic Eros and the Beauty of Imperfection Topic Sentence: Busi & Ebgi note that Pico’s angelic eros retains a Platonic erotic dynamism : beauty and imperfection belong to creation, not to God; the angels’ love expresses the world’s continual striving toward the divine. Explanation: In Giovanni Pico della Mirandola: Mito, magia, Qabbalah (Einaudi 2014), Busi and Ebgi stress that even in Pico’s most scholastic writings the language of eros never disappears. His angels are not static intellects but lovers of God , whose desire marks the ontological distance between Creator and creation. “In the angelic mind burns a desire without lack—an eros that is motion toward the source, the very rhythm of created being. Beauty, in Pico’s cosmology, is not divine perfection but the shining trace of imperfection: it reveals that the world still tends toward its principle.” — Busi & Ebgi, Mito, magia, Qabbalah , pp. 118–119 They cite Heptaplus II.3, where Pico explains that God’s light is reflected through the angelic hierarchies: “The divine beauty flows into the angels as rays into mirrors, and from them descends into the soul, which, seeing that beauty, is inflamed with love.” Thus, angelic eros mediates between transcendence and immanence. Angels “love by understanding and understand by loving”—a formula that keeps alive the Platonic fusion of eros and noēsis . “Pico’s angels do not annul the sensual in favor of the intelligible; they sublimate it. The erotic tension of the cosmos remains, but it is transfigured into intellectual desire.” — Busi & Ebgi, p. 121 This insight links the Commento ’s Venus allegory to the Heptaplus ’s angelology: love is the energy of creation itself , the mark of its contingency and its aspiration toward divine unity. 🜍 Synthesis Scholar Focus Key Quotation Effect on Pico’s Angelology Howlett (2019) Aristotelian metaphysics “The angelic intellect is the purest created form in which divine act is received without distortion.” Angels express energeia as intelligible act rather than pneuma; miracle becomes ontology. Edelheit (2008) Thomist–Dionysian theology “The angelic intellect … the first created participation in divine energeia .” Angels are models of pure act; intellect = participation in divine operation. Busi & Ebgi (2014) Platonic erotic metaphysics “In the angelic mind burns a desire without lack—an eros that is motion toward the source.” Angels embody beauty-in-motion; creation’s imperfection is its longing for God. In sum Pico’s angelic intellect is the nexus of his metaphysical synthesis: For Howlett , it naturalizes the miraculous through Aristotelian act. For Edelheit , it theologizes intellect as participation in divine energy. For Busi & Ebgi , it eroticizes creation : angels are living acts of desire and understanding, whose beauty signals both the world’s contingency and its luminous participation in God.

I’ll break this down into phases, integrating paraphrase, analysis, and direct quotations to show how his poetry evolves from courtly eroticism to prophetic reform theology . 🜏 I. The Early Medicean Poet (c. 1470–1485) Context: Benivieni began as a lyric poet in Lorenzo de’ Medici’s Florence — a friend of Poliziano and a member of the Platonic Academy. He was admired as a cantore amoroso and composer of songs for public and private performance. Themes and Style His earliest poems — love canzoni, sonnets, and strambotti — are modeled on Petrarch and Poliziano, but already inflected by Ficino’s De amore . They celebrate ideal beauty ( bellezza ) and harmonious measure ( dolcezza e consonanza ), often using musical metaphors: “Love’s flame tunes my heart to the lyre of Venus; in concord my soul sounds its sweet lament.” ( Rime giovanili , paraphrase) Sherry Roush describes these as “courtly, musical, and mathematical in tone — a poetry of harmony both physical and metaphysical.” Music is the first sign of Benivieni’s lifelong fascination with order as beauty , which will later turn theological. 🜏 II. Pico’s Influence and the Canzone dell’amor celeste e divino (c. 1485–1486) The Turning Point: Benivieni’s Canzone dell’amor celeste e divino (“Song of Heavenly and Divine Love”) marks his first transformation: it is the poem that inspired Pico’s Commento sopra una canzone d’amore . Structure and Allegory The canzone allegorizes love as the soul’s ascent from the sensual to the divine. It contrasts: Amore terreno (earthly love): bound to sense, time, and beauty of form; Amore celeste e divino : the intellectual love that unites the soul to the “first beauty” ( prima bellezza ). Benivieni writes (trans. from Garin’s edition): “Love, whose origin is in Heaven, leads our mind from the fair things we see below to the eternal light, the fountain of all beauty.” Philosophical Background Olga Zorzi Pugliese notes that Benivieni fuses Platonic eros with Christian intellectus : “In Benivieni’s poetry the movement of love is no longer purely Neoplatonic ascent; it is an Aristotelian motion of the intellect from potency to act, reinterpreted as grace.” ( Pugliese, 2003, p. 342 ) Pico’s Commento treats this poem as a theologia amoris — decoding Venus as angelic intellect and Cupid as divine eros. Thus Benivieni’s lyric becomes the seed of Pico’s metaphysical project , transforming erotic desire into energeia divina . 🜏 III. The Commento sopra a più sue canzoni e sonetti dello amore e della bellezza divina (1500) After Pico’s death, Benivieni reissued his earlier love poems with a new commentary that reinterprets them as religious allegories . He insists that his youthful verses had “always been intended for divine subjects,” but that now he wishes “to make explicit what was once veiled in shadow.” “Love and beauty are not deceitful idols, but mirrors of divine light. I have sought in these rimes to lead the reader from the visible to the invisible, from the creature to the Creator.” ( Proemio , 1500 edition) Pugliese explains: “Benivieni’s commentary consciously imitates Pico’s method. He Christianizes the Platonic scheme but preserves its structure: beauty remains the splendor of truth, and love the intellect’s movement toward God.” (p. 343) Tone: While the Canzone had been lyrical and intellectual, the 1500 Commento is didactic and penitential — a confessional rewriting of his poetic past. It represents his inward conversion from philosopher-lover to contemplative Christian. 🜏 IV. The Savonarolan Phase (c. 1495–1506) Historical Context: By the 1490s, Benivieni had joined the circle of Girolamo Savonarola, the Dominican preacher of reform and apocalyptic renewal. He recanted his youthful “pagan” verse, rewrote many poems in devotional forms, and composed canzoni spirituali for the bonfires and public processions. Thematic Shift In his Two Poems on Spiritual Renewal (English trans. 2002, ed. Sherry Roush), Benivieni prefaces his verse with this confession: “I once sang of earthly beauty, but now I sing of divine love and the holy fire that renews the city of Florence.” The first poem, A Canzone on the Bliss Promised to Florence , celebrates the city as the chosen instrument of God’s reform: “O city more fortunate than all others, raised high by God’s right hand, thou art the temple of His law renewed.” ( Two Poems , p. 19) The second poem, A Canzone on the Bonfire of Vanities , exhorts citizens to burn their worldly ornaments, transmuting the allegory of ascent through fire into literal civic purgation. Roush observes: “Benivieni reworks the Platonic fire of eros into Savonarola’s eschatological flame; the vocabulary of purification, ascent, and light remains, but the context shifts from intellectual to moral salvation.” ( Renaissance Quarterly , 55:1 [2002], p. 90) Poetic Style Even in reform mode, Benivieni preserves his lyrical cadence and symbolism: “Not harsh, but bright is this fire that consumes; it kindles hearts with love, not with wrath.” ( Two Poems , p. 27) This blending of devotional zeal and aesthetic elegance made him the “official poet of the Piagnoni ,” the Savonarolan faithful. 🜏 V. The Dantean and Prophetic Benivieni (1506–1520) In Il Cantico in laude di Dante (1506), Benivieni merges his Pico–Savonarola heritage into a single prophetic vision. The poem, a long terza rima vision published as a preface to a Florentine edition of the Divine Comedy , is written as a dream-encounter with Dante. Visionary Encounter He meets the shade of Dante, who reveals that Savonarola’s preaching fulfills Dante’s moral prophecy: “Behold, my city Florence — once blind, now purged by fire. The prophet rises from my ashes, teaching men to love justice and despise the world.” ( Cantico , paraphrased by Roush, p. 83) Roush explains: “Benivieni uses Dante’s authority as a mask through which to speak his own Savonarolan politics. Dante, the poet of exile, becomes the mouthpiece of reform.” (p. 85) Poetic Voice The tone blends humility with civic confidence: “I have seen the river Arno turn to crystal, and angels walk among its waters — O Florence, renewed in flame, rejoice!” ( Cantico , paraphrase) Thus Benivieni’s Cantico completes the transfiguration of the Florentine lyric tradition — from dolce stil novo to prophetic theocracy. 🜏 VI. Stylistic and Philosophical Evolution — Summary Table Phase Representative Works Themes Philosophical Grounding Tone / Style Medicean Youth (1470s–80s) Rime giovanili Sensual beauty, harmony, music, number Ficinian Platonism Elegant, musical, courtly Pichian Ascent (1485–90) Canzone dell’amor celeste e divino Love as ascent; divine beauty; angelic intellect Pico’s metaphysical eros Lyrical and allegorical Conversion Commentary (1500) Commento sopra a più sue canzoni e sonetti Repentance; intellectual love transformed into faith Thomist & Dionysian theology via Pico Didactic, reflective Savonarolan Reform (1495–1506) Two Poems on Spiritual Renewal Florence as New Jerusalem; purification by fire Apocalyptic Christianity Prophetic, musical, civic Dantean Prophecy (1506–20) Cantico in laude di Dante Dante as prophet; Savonarola’s fulfillment Neo-Augustinian theology Visionary, epic-prophetic 🜏 VII. Thematic Synthesis Benivieni’s poetic journey can be read as a triple conversion : 1. From Venus to Sophia — the transformation of erotic beauty into divine wisdom. > “From the love that wounds to the love that heals; from the eye’s delight to the mind’s illumination.” ( Commento , paraphrase) 2. From Apollo to Jeremiah — from pagan lyric to Christian prophecy. > “Not for delight I sing, but for the correction of my people.” ( Two Poems , p. 25) 3. From Harmony to Fire — aesthetic order redefined as purgative grace. > “Music and flame are one: the world must burn to become melody.” (Savonarolan canzone, paraphrased) Scholarly Consensus Pugliese (2003): “Benivieni spiritualized Pico’s Platonism without extinguishing its eros; he is the mediator between Platonic allegory and Christian devotion.” Roush (2002): “His late poetry is both civic and visionary — an appropriation of Dante’s authority to preach the politics of moral renewal.” Motta (2005): “In Benivieni, poetry becomes the vernacular theology of the Piagnone conscience: emotional, learned, penitential.” Conclusion Girolamo Benivieni’s poetry traces the most complete moral and metaphysical arc of the Florentine Renaissance: from the harmonious beauty of Ficino , through the intellectual eros of Pico , to the prophetic fire of Savonarola , and finally into the Dantean ideal of the poet as moral legislator . His corpus, read as a whole, is a living map of how the poet-lover of the early Renaissance became the poet-prophet of the Christian humanist age — and how lyric beauty was transformed into an instrument of spiritual energeia .

“In Benivieni’s poetry the movement of love is no longer purely Neoplatonic ascent; it is an Aristotelian motion of the intellect from potency to act, reinterpreted as grace.” (Pugliese, 2003, p. 342)

Exactly — that sentence from Olga Zorzi Pugliese’s 2003 article (“ Girolamo Benivieni, amico e collaboratore di Giovanni Pico della Mirandola ,” Bibliothèque d’Humanisme et Renaissance , vol. 65 no. 2) captures the philosophical core of Benivieni’s transformation under Pico’s influence. Let’s unpack it in full context, with supporting passages from both Pugliese’s essay and Benivieni’s own poems. 🜍 1. What Pugliese Means by “No Longer Purely Neoplatonic Ascent” In traditional Neoplatonic eros (as in Ficino’s De amore or Plotinus’s Enneads ), love is a linear ascent from sense to intellect — the soul moves upward by stripping away the material, finally uniting with the divine One through contemplative vision. Benivieni begins there, but Pugliese argues that under Pico’s guidance he recasts this upward motion in Aristotelian terms — as an immanent process of act and potency rather than a metaphysical ladder of separation. “Benivieni’s Canzone dell’amor celeste e divino takes the Platonic scheme of ascent and subjects it to Pico’s synthesis of Aristotle and Plato. The movement of love is no longer pure escape from the world but the intellect’s self-actualization in grace.” — Pugliese (2003), p. 342 Thus, love becomes formative rather than fugitive : the mind doesn’t flee the material but perfects its own potency within it, by the act of divine illumination. 🜍 2. Pico’s Aristotelian Framework as Benivieni’s Template Pico had argued in De ente et uno and in his Heptaplus that the universe is ordered by act and potency , not by a hierarchy of descending pneuma. Pugliese shows that Benivieni absorbs this metaphysical grammar , turning love into a creative act rather than a mystical separation: “The poet follows Pico’s anthropology: the human soul is a middle nature capable of transforming potency into act. The motion of love thus mirrors the metaphysical structure of creation itself — the unfolding of divine act in matter.” — Pugliese (ibid.) This is why Benivieni’s mature poetry (the 1500 Commento sopra a più sue canzoni e sonetti ) insists that love and grace are two aspects of the same energeia . In one key passage, he glosses his own earlier verse: “Love is the divine power that draws the soul to imitate God’s act. When we love rightly, our potential intellect becomes light in act, because God’s grace operates in it.” — Commento sopra a più sue canzoni , bk. I (ch. on Amor celeste ) Here Benivieni redefines love’s “motion” as an Aristotelian transition from potentiality to actuality , reinterpreted theologically as sanctification — the soul’s actualization by grace. 🜍 3. The Theological Twist: Grace as Energeia Where Ficino’s ascent ends in intellectual vision , Benivieni’s ends in grace . Pugliese stresses that the poet’s collaboration with Pico infused his erotic philosophy with Christian metaphysics of participation : the soul’s movement is not self-generated but caused by divine act. “Benivieni’s poetry converts Platonic eros into theological energeia . The act that completes the soul’s potency is not its own intelligence but grace itself — the divine operation that informs and perfects human love.” — Pugliese (2003), p. 343 This is a decisive innovation: it keeps the structure of Platonic desire but redefines its teleology — fulfillment comes not through contemplative autonomy but through infused activity , God acting within intellect and love. 🜍 4. A Textual Illustration: Canzone dell’amor celeste e divino In the poem’s climactic stanzas Benivieni writes (trans. from the Garin edition): *Love leads the soul from low to high, From the shadow to the sun’s clear light; It kindles thought with living flame, Till mind becomes what it beholds.* This is exactly the movement Pugliese describes: not the negation of desire, but its transformation into intellectual act — a motion of energeia . Here, love “kindles thought” — an Aristotelian verb of actualization — until intellect itself becomes luminous, the hallmark of divine operation ( actus Dei ). 🜍 5. Relation to Pico’s Theology of Act Pico’s Oratio de hominis dignitate states that God created man “with the seeds of all forms” so that he might “fashion himself into what he wills.” Benivieni poetically transposes that same idea into his theology of love: “Amor celeste … fa l’uom simile a Dio, non per fuga ma per operazione.” (“Heavenly love makes man like God, not by flight but by operation.”) This is almost a vernacular paraphrase of Pico’s metaphysical anthropology. Love = the act ( energeia ) through which potential intellect is perfected by divine likeness. 🜍 6. Scholarly Perspective Pugliese’s insight connects Benivieni’s poetics to the broader Renaissance shift from emanationist mysticism to Aristotelian metaphysics of participation — the same current we see in Pico’s angels and in Howlett’s “Aristotelianization of the miraculous.” Concept Ficino’s Model Benivieni’s Revision (per Pugliese) Love’s Direction Upward ascent of soul through spheres Interior motion of intellect toward act Mechanism Pneumatic sympathy, contemplative vision Grace as divine act informing intellect Goal Union with the One through vision Participation in God through activity Philosophical Grammar Neoplatonic hierarchy Aristotelian act/potency reinterpreted theologically In Pugliese’s own words: “In Benivieni’s poetry the movement of love is no longer purely Neoplatonic ascent; it is an Aristotelian motion of the intellect from potency to act, reinterpreted as grace. The ascent is now an immanent actualization — the fulfillment of human intellect in divine operation.” — ( Pugliese, 2003, p. 342 ) In Short Benivieni’s verse converts Platonic longing into metaphysical activity . His love poetry is not escapist mysticism but the lyric of energeia — a poetics of transformation where the intellect, moved by grace, passes from potency to act. Pugliese’s formulation perfectly captures how Benivieni becomes the vernacular poet of Pico’s Christian-Aristotelian metaphysics.

Beautifully observed — that cluster of ideas is the metaphysical heart of Pico’s angelology in the Heptaplus . Let’s unpack that passage and the surrounding logic in its textual, philosophical, and theological context , showing how it ties together Aristotle, Dionysius, and Pico’s anthropology. 🜍 1. The Text: Heptaplus I.iii — the Angelic Intellect as Composite Pico writes (in Garin’s Latin text, trans. paraphrased): “The eye, that is, intellectual substance, is not wholly simple… for angels are composed of act and potency, although there is dispute as to what the act is, and what the potency.” — Heptaplus , I.iii This remark appears amid his exegesis of Genesis 1:2–3 (“The Spirit of God was borne upon the waters… and God said: Let there be light”). Pico identifies “the waters” as the angelic intellect — the first created spiritual nature — and “the light” as the illumination of divine forms within that intellect. So the angelic mind is not actus purus (pure act) like God; rather, it is a composite being that receives act . It is potential in respect to divine illumination , but actual in its own intellectual operation. 🜍 2. The Aristotelian Background — Act and Potency in Pure Spirits Pico here engages a long-standing scholastic problem: Are angels composed of matter and form? Aquinas (ST I.50–54) had argued that they are not, but that they are composites of essence and existence — thus composed of act and potency in a metaphysical sense . Pico echoes this: Angels are not corporeal, yet they are not utterly simple. Their act is their intellectual activity; Their potency is their capacity to receive divine illumination. Hence, angels embody the first created participation in divine energeia — a finite form of infinite act. “For they are lights, yet not light itself; intelligences, yet not intellect itself. They behold by participation what God beholds by essence.” — Pico, Heptaplus , I.iii That distinction between seeing by participation and seeing by essence is the line between Creator and creature, act and potency. 🜍 3. The Human Analogue — Man as Angel in Potency Immediately following the angelic discussion, Pico turns to man, created “after the image of God” but placed between angel and animal. He thereby mirrors the angelic state in potency . Human intellect ( intellectus possibilis ) is potential until it receives illumination from God, just as the angelic intellect was dark waters until God said, “Let there be light.” “The human mind is a mirror of the angelic; when illumined, it becomes all things in act, and by contemplation ascends to its exemplar.” — Heptaplus , II.i Here we see Pico blending: Aristotle’s psychology ( De anima III.5: intellect in potency becomes intellect in act through light); Dionysius’s theology of illumination (angels transmit divine light in a hierarchical chain); and Augustine’s idea of divine illumination as grace. Thus, human cognition is an angelic process in miniature : intellect as potency → illumination as act → contemplation as participation. 🜍 4. Participation in God’s Energeia In Aristotelian language, energeia is the actuality of a form. In Dionysian and Pico’s usage, it becomes divine activity — the act of knowing and loving that radiates from God into creation . Pico sees angels as the first beings who live by that act : their existence is the continuous operation of contemplation. “They live by understanding; to know is their being. In their knowledge God’s act shines forth as light in mirrors.” — Heptaplus , I.iii Humans participate in that act through: 1. Contemplation — the intellect’s union with divine truth, which perfects its potency; 2. Love — the will’s attraction to that truth, which imitates angelic charity. So when you write: “It achieves participation in God’s energeia through contemplation and love,” that is exactly Pico’s fusion of Aristotelian psychology and Christian theology: the intellect’s motion toward act is graced activity , not natural self-activation. 🜍 5. Reason as the Mediator — The Human Triplex Structure In the Heptaplus and Oratio , Pico articulates a tripartite anthropology : Level Faculty Mode of Operation Analogy Sense Imagination / phantasia Receives images Body Reason Discursive intellect Mediates, judges Soul Intellect Contemplation Receives illumination Angel “Reason is the ladder of the intellect, standing between the shadows of sense and the splendor of pure thought.” — Heptaplus , II.ii Hence, reason mediates between sense and intellect , enabling ascent toward the angelic state. In Aristotelian terms, reason abstracts forms from sense; in Pico’s synthesis, it spiritualizes sense — a process completed by divine light. 🜍 6. Theological Implication — Angels and Humans in One Economy of Grace Pico’s move is to collapse the ontological hierarchy of Neoplatonism into a theological continuum of act and grace : Angels are created act (intellects already illuminated). Humans are potential act (intellects awaiting illumination). Both depend on God’s energeia as first cause and ongoing motion. This replaces the old theurgic or pneumatic intermediaries with a single metaphysical grammar: act perfecting potency through illumination . “That the divine light shines upon the angel and through him upon man signifies the single order of grace by which all intellects are made active.” — Heptaplus , II.iii 🜍 7. Synthesis — The Chain of Act Ontological Level State Act / Potency Relation Mode of Divine Presence God Actus purus No potency Energeia ipsa — the divine act itself Angels Created intellects Act relative to matter; potency relative to God Receive act as light; live by contemplation Humans Potential intellects Potency relative to truth Seek illumination through reason, love, and grace Nature Corporeal Passive potency Reflects divine act symbolically This scheme is Pico’s full “Aristotelianization of the miraculous” : the divine act flows down through being as graded energeia , and illumination replaces magic as the mode of divine causation. In Short “The eye, that is, intellectual substance, is not wholly simple… angels are composed of act and potency.” Therefore — - The angelic mind is the first created reflection of divine energeia : pure intellect in continual act of contemplation. - The human intellect mirrors it as potency seeking act : illuminated by God’s light through reason and love. - Reason mediates between sense and intellect, enabling ascent into the angelic operation. In Pico’s Christian-Aristotelian cosmos, both angels and humans exist as living energies of divine act , their illumination being the grace that makes potential intellect actual — the energeia of understanding and love that constitutes being itself.

Here’s a proposed MA thesis outline synthesizing the newest scholarship on Pico della Mirandola’s magic , Kabbalah , and angelic metaphysics , showing how to move beyond the old “proto-scientific magus” interpretation (Yates, Walker) toward an understanding of Pico’s theological anthropology and metaphysics of intellect . Title “Knowing How Causes Interlock Under God’s Design: Giovanni Pico della Mirandola’s Angelic Metaphysics Between Magic, Kabbalah, and Aristotelian Act” Abstract (200 words) This thesis argues that Pico della Mirandola’s philosophy of magic and Kabbalah articulates a Christian metaphysics of intellectual freedom , rather than a proto-scientific naturalism. Drawing on Pico’s Conclusiones Magicae et Cabalisticae , Heptaplus , De Ente et Uno , and Commento sopra una canzone d’amore , the study interprets his “magus” as a philosopher-theologian who perceives the divine grammar of creation. Engaging modern reassessments by Brian Copenhaver (2022) , Sophia Howlett (2019) , Amos Edelheit (2008) , and Giulio Busi & Raphael Ebgi (2014) , it shows how Pico’s angelic metaphysics reconciles Aristotelian energeia with Dionysian illumination, locating the magus not in pneumatic manipulation but in contemplative intellection. The result is a picture of magia naturalis as philosophical mediation between act and potency, matter and form, nature and grace — a theological anthropology that anticipates Renaissance debates about intellect, providence, and human dignity. Chapter Outline Chapter 1 – The Historiography of the Magus: From Yates to Copenhaver Review of Frances Yates’s “Hermetic Magus” thesis. Copenhaver’s Magic and the Dignity of Man (2019) reframing Pico’s “Oration” as rhetorical theology , not an occult manifesto【191:0†(Critical Political Theory and Radical Practice) Sophia Howlett†L37-L39】. Farmer’s Syncretism in the West on Pico’s textual context, rejecting the “angelic amulet” hypothesis【191:5†(Medieval & Renaissance Texts & Studies 167) Stephen A. Farmer†L49-L63】. Rabin’s analysis of magic as theoretical natural philosophy, not operative technique【191:10†M. V. Dougherty – New Essays†L23-L39】. Chapter 2 – The Angelic Intellect: Energeia and Illumination Reading De Ente et Uno and Heptaplus : “The eye, that is, intellectual substance, is not wholly simple… angels are composed of act and potency.” Edelheit’s claim: Pico’s angels are models of pure energeia , mediating divine act into created being. Howlett’s argument: this is Pico’s “Aristotelianization of the miraculous” , turning divine act into metaphysical structure within creation. Busi & Ebgi: angelic eros reflects creation’s dynamic imperfection —beauty belongs to the world, not to God. Chapter 3 – Kabbalah as Grammar of Creation Analysis of Conclusiones Cabalisticae and Pico’s claim: “There is no science that assures us more of the divinity of Christ than magic and Cabala.”【191:1†M. V. Dougherty – New Essays†L5-L7】 How Kabbalistic language operates as a symbolic logic of divine causation . Integration of Maimonides, Abulafia, and Alemanno (Copenhaver 2006; Ogren 2010) into Pico’s hermeneutics of the Name and linguistic creation【191:8†Ogren – The Beginning of the World†L25-L34】. Farmer on Pico’s synthesis of sefirot with Dionysian orders of angels — a Christian Kabbalistic metaphysics of procession 【191:13†Farmer – Syncretism in the West†L5-L15】. Chapter 4 – Natural Magic and Theological Anthropology Close reading of Conclusiones Magicae (II.9): “Magicam operari non est aliud quam maritare mundum.” Pico’s natural magic as the form of knowing how causes interlock under divine providence . Howlett and Akopyan: natural magic as consummation of scientia naturalis and a prefiguration of Pico’s later critique of astrology【191:6†Akopyan – Debating the Stars†L13-L23】. Pico’s rejection of spiritus mundi and talismanic power: magic as intellectual participation in divine act (Farmer, 1998)【191:7†Farmer – Syncretism in the West†L75-L77】. Chapter 5 – Hermeneutics of Light: From Angels to the Magus Linking Pico’s angelology with his Christian Kabbalistic semiotics of divine names. The magus as contemplative rather than operator: “The Spirit of the Lord was borne upon the deep… and light was made in the angel.” Synthesis of Aristotelian act/potency with Dionysian illumination: intellect as the marriage of heaven and earth. Chapter 6 – Toward a Christian Metaphysics of Freedom The magus as the free human intellect mediating creation’s act and potential. Pico’s magic as a theological anthropology of freedom , not domination of nature. Implications for early modern science: from “natural magic” to experimental metaphysics. Methodology Textual exegesis of Pico’s Latin works in Garin’s editions ( Opera omnia , Scritti vari ). Comparative analysis with Aquinas ( Summa theologiae I.105–111), Dionysius, Proclus, Maimonides, and Abulafia. Reception study through Howlett, Edelheit, Busi, Copenhaver, and Farmer. Expected Contribution Reframes Pico’s magus as an Aristotelian–Dionysian theologian of act, illumination, and language. Bridges intellectual history (magic/Kabbalah) and metaphysics (act/potency, energeia). Provides a theological rather than occultist genealogy for Renaissance “natural philosophy.” Would you like me to draft an annotated bibliography and research design (chapters + primary texts + secondary reading plan by semester) next? It would make this into a working graduate proposal ready for submission.

Great question. Short version: Pico uses Proclus as a precision tool —a rigorously architected map of intelligible → intellectual → super-mundane orders, built out of triads, henads, and graded causality—to (a) organize his universal concord ( prisca theologia ), and (b) push back on Ficino’s Plotinus-heavy, spiritus-psychological reading of ascent. Where Ficino leans on a broadly Plotinian ladder (and spiritus ), Pico deploys Proclean structure to relocate ascent into intelligible act and Christian illumination , not pneumatic magic. Below I sketch (I) why Proclus, (II) how that critiques Ficino, then (III) read key Proclean Conclusiones you listed as Pico’s “working Proclus.” 1) A finer-grained Platonism than Plotinus. Proclus supplies a tiered cosmos —intelligible / intellectual / supermundane—governed by triads (remaining–procession–return) and henads (divine ones) that let Pico correlate Plato’s Parmenides + Phaedrus + mythic theologies with Christian angelology and Kabbalah. Farmer shows Pico repeatedly cross-wires Orphic, Dionysian, and Kabbalistic orders via Proclean machinery (e.g., “guardians” ↔ Dionysian “powers” ↔ Gevurah). fileciteturn191file13L9-L16 fileciteturn191file9L52-L64 2) A universal correlational grammar. Pico wants to synchronize Greek mythic theonyms, Platonic loci, Dionysian angelic ranks, and Hebrew sefirot. Proclus’ taxonomy gives him the slots and rules to do that without collapsing distinctions—hence the elaborate “deorum” trinitates, ebdomades, and mediations in your list. Farmer’s commentary on the 900 Theses repeatedly tracks exactly these Proclean correlations. fileciteturn191file5L1-L11 fileciteturn191file9L49-L66 3) From “occult sympathies” to structured causality . In the newer reading, Pico’s “natural magic” is the intellectual discernment of causal orders —how effects “remain, proceed, return”—not talismanic operations. Farmer underlines: no talismanic magic in the theses; spiritus mundi isn’t Pico’s framework; the emphasis is exegetical and metaphysical . fileciteturn191file7L49-L77 4) Against a one-speed Plotinian ascent. Ficino’s De amore rides a Plotinian psychology of ascent through imagination/ spiritus . Pico (as Michael J. B. Allen shows) re-reads Venus/Orpheus to insist you must separate from phantasia to see Ideas—in other words, relocate love/beauty to intelligible act (Uranian Venus), not pneumatic ladders. That’s part of the “beef.” fileciteturn15file8L6-L16 5) Proclus gives Pico the scaffolding Ficino’s Plotinus lacks. The Proclean triads, henads, and graded gods/angels let Pico specify where and how ascent occurs (intellect → supermundane), which mediations operate, and how Christian faith/love/intellect crown the ascent (see concl. 24.43–44 below). Allen frames Pico’s corrections as pointed interventions in Ficino’s Platonism. fileciteturn15file14L47-L53 6) A better bridge to Dionysius and Kabbalah. Proclus’ middle terms and series let Pico align pagan henads with Dionysian orders and sefirot without reducing one to the other—a move Farmer details (and moderns later used to unmask Pseudo-Dionysius’s late antique borrowings). fileciteturn191file13L11-L19 Below, quick glosses showing what each cluster does for Pico’s program: (24.1) Limit & unlimited (intelligible) → male & female (intellectual), identity/alterity (supermundane), sameness/otherness in soul. → Pico imports Parmenidean and Platonic polarities as order-specific principles ; ascent means learning the right difference at each level (no generic “upward yearning”). (Pairs track Proclean triadic logic.) (24.2) Saturnian, Jovian, fatal laws; Adrasteia over all. → Lawfulness is tiered ; Providence/Fate distribute causality through orders. This replaces “occult sympathies” with jurisdictions —a Proclean legal metaphysics. (24.3) Who gets called “god.” → Analogical predication across orders: absolute to the One; essential to intellectual; participation/contact/similitude down through angels, daemons, human souls. That’s Pico’s lexicon for concord . (24.5) No number in the intelligible (only multitude & parental causes of numbers); number exists “according to essence” in the intellectual. → Classic Proclean arithmology : number emerges as you descend—handy for Pico’s Cabalistic numeri/characteres without crude arithmetic literalism. (24.10–11) What intellectual gods “take” from above; production modes: intelligible produce uniformly; intelligible-intellectual trinally; intellectual heptadically. → A Proclean production grammar Pico uses to correlate Plato’s myths with Christian angelology (and, via Farmer, Orphic/Dionysian/Kabbalistic series). fileciteturn191file9L29-L36 (24.12–16) Rhea mediates Saturn/Jove; Curetes purify paternal work; “tragic slayings” signify the 7th discrete unity; Rhea as Ceres producing orders. → Mythic names encode functions (mediation, purification, differentiation). This is how Pico reads myths as metaphysics , not stories. (24.17) “All things are in all, each in its mode.” → The Proclean axiom enabling Pico’s cross-tradition equivalences (Orphic guardians = Dionysian powers = Gevurah, etc.). fileciteturn191file9L52-L64 (24.18–19) Paternal only in intelligibles; “factive/operative” in the new gods; every operation has immanent and transeunt faces. → Causation is two-way marked (immanent/transeunt)—Pico’s way to discipline “magic” into metaphysical action (no free-floating “influences”). (24.20–26) Supermundane gods exempt from the parts of the universe; their property is to assimilate/communicate; Jupiter/Neptune/Pluto as substantifying/vivifying/convertive; they receive Saturn’s rule via a “maker Jupiter.” → A causal chain binding mythic names to ontological jobs (substance, life, conversion)—a Proclean alternative to Plotinian generalities. (24.27–29) Each ducal hypostasis covers domains (being, life, conversion) and cosmic regions. → A cosmic map for correlations (astral, elemental, psychic) without lapsing into astral determinism—useful for Pico’s anti-astrology trajectory (Akopyan). fileciteturn191file11L9-L17 (24.30–35) Proserpina’s triad (Diana/Persephone/Minerva… Hecate/Anima/Virtus); 12 gods of Phaedrus as the bond between super- and sub-mundane, in four triads (opificative, custoditive, vital, conversive). → Proclean “middle terms” that let Pico anchor Platonic myth, Christian angelology, and natural philosophy without Ficinian spiritus . (24.36–37) Four armies of younger gods with proportional armies of angels/daemons/souls. → The place where Farmer’s angelic–henadic equivalences become explicit: it underwrites Pico’s bold “same-in-different-order” identifications. fileciteturn191file13L9-L16 (24.43–46) Ascent to the Lord through intellect, love, faith; true faith is “supersubstantial,” joining us to God beyond intellect; not every soul participates in intellect (but all participate in One). → Pico’s Christian capstone on Proclus: ascent culminates not in Plotinian psychology or “pneuma,” but faith/charity/intellect —a move consistent with the newer “theological anthropology” reading (Howlett; Edelheit). fileciteturn191file0L37-L39 (24.48–49) First triad remains; second remains & proceeds; third converts after procession. → The Proclean mónē–próodos–epistrophē schema—Pico’s engine for reading Genesis, Plato, and Dionysius in one grammar. (24.55) Where to seek: perfected intellect from intelligibles; uplifting power from intellectuals; unmixed operation from the super-mundane; angelic choirs fill us with true “aspiration” from the gods. → A map of practices ordered to levels—why Pico’s “natural magic” is a hierarchy-sensitive intellection , not talismanry (Farmer). fileciteturn191file7L49-L77 The upshot Yes —Proclus is central to Pico’s critique of Ficino’s Plotinian bias . He lets Pico relocate “love/beauty/ascent” from spiritus and imagination to intelligible act , architected by Proclean triads and closed by Christian faith–love–intellect . That’s exactly the pivot the newer scholarship tracks: from occultism to metaphysics , from pneumatic chemistry to theological anthropology of intellect —with Proclus as the structural backbone and Farmer/Allen documenting how Pico “wires” it across Orphic/Dionysian/Kabbalistic materials. fileciteturn15file14L47-L53 fileciteturn191file7L49-L77 fileciteturn191file9L49-L66 If you’d like, I can turn this into a side-by-side table (Ficino Plotinian ladder vs. Pico Proclean architecture) with the exact thesis numbers and the best modern commentaries keyed to each.

Pico’s Plotinian conclusions (Conclusiones secundum Plotinum, I.20.1–15) embody a selective and critical engagement with Plotinus that exposes both his continuity with Ficino’s Neoplatonism and his decisive shift toward an Aristotelianized Christian metaphysics. Several key scholarly perspectives—Farmer, Allen, Howlett, and Dougherty—help unpack how Pico transforms Plotinus’s Enneads into a theology of intellectual freedom, hierarchy, and deification. 1. Plotinus’s Doctrines in Pico’s Hands Stephen Farmer notes that Pico composed these fifteen Plotinian theses before Ficino’s translation of the Enneads was completed, relying instead on Greek and Arabic intermediaries (Porphyry, Iamblichus, “Adeland the Arab”)【205:3†(Medieval & Renaissance Texts & Studies 167)†L13-L19】. The early theses—“ Primum intelligibile non est extra primum intellectum ” and “ Non tota descendit anima quum descendit ”—mark his alignment with Plotinus’s ontology of emanation while already signaling his preference for a participatory metaphysics: intellect and intelligible coincide within divine act, not as separate hypostases. Farmer observes that Pico’s Plotinian section is “much more perfunctory” than his elaborate Proclean theses, revealing that Plotinus was for Pico a theological resource rather than a systematic model 【205:3†(Medieval & Renaissance Texts & Studies 167)†L48-L54】. The Plotinus theses affirm the immortality of all life (20.3), the hierarchy of soul (20.4–5), and the coincidence of being, life, and intellect (20.6) as a triune structure of existence—concepts Pico integrates into his Christianized doctrine of felicity (20.7). 2. The Ascent of the Intellect and Felicity Thesis 20.7— “Foelicitas hominis ultima est cum particularis intellectus noster totali primoque intellectui plene coniungitur” —epitomizes Pico’s transformation of Plotinian henosis into scholastic idiom. As Farmer and Howlett note, this union of the individual with the “total intellect” reframes mystical absorption into God as participation in energeia , not dissolution into unity【205:15†(Medieval & Renaissance Texts & Studies 167)†L31-L33】【205:10†(Critical Political Theory and Radical Practice)†L10-L19】. Howlett connects this to Pico’s Aristotelian notion of the intellectus agens : felicity is the actualization of intellect’s potential through divine illumination—the human reflection of angelic act. Thus, the Plotinian felicity thesis anticipates Pico’s later anthropology in the Oratio and Heptaplus : man’s freedom lies in his capacity to rise by intellect toward the angelic and divine, actualizing within himself all the forms of being. 3. Virtue and Deification Theses 20.8–9—rejecting civic virtue as “virtues simpliciter” and claiming that assimilation to God through virtue is only preparatory—distill Plotinus’s mystical quietism into Christian moral theology. Farmer observes that Pico interprets Plotinian virtue hierarchically: lower virtues are merely ethical dispositions, while higher contemplation realizes participation in God’s act itself【205:15†(Medieval & Renaissance Texts & Studies 167)†L52-L56】. This complements his later magia naturalis doctrines, where knowledge of causal order mirrors divine providence. 4. Allen and Dougherty: Plotinus in the Ficino Debate Michael J. B. Allen shows that Pico weaponized Plotinus against Ficino in the Commento and De ente et uno , particularly around the statement that “God understands nothing.” Pico corrects Ficino’s reading by insisting that Plotinus meant God possesses intellect causally, not formally —that divine thought is the cause of all understanding, not an act within it【205:4†(Variorum Collected Studies 1063)†L12-L18】. This recasting of Plotinian apophaticism as causal metaphysics turns the mystical ascent into an Aristotelianized doctrine of efficient causation, grounding divine simplicity in act ( energeia ) rather than negation. Dougherty similarly notes that both Ficino and Pico “celebrated philosophically the birthday of Venus” but diverged on the metaphysics of ascent: Ficino’s unity of love versus Pico’s intellectual causality of being 【205:1†M. V. Dougherty – Pico della Mirandola: New Essays†L5-L19】. 5. Howlett: Plotinus and the Active Intellect Sophia Howlett situates Pico’s Plotinian theses within his Aristotelian anthropology: the intellect’s ascent from potency to act is both ethical and metaphysical. “For Pico,” she writes, “true freedom is based on the reasoned employment of the intellect to overcome the tyranny of the will”【205:9†(Critical Political Theory and Radical Practice)†L42-L46】. Thus the Plotinian identification of being, life, and intellect (20.6) becomes, in Pico, an anthropology of energeia : the human soul mirrors the eternal procession of divine act through understanding. 6. Synthesis: Pico’s Christianized Plotinus In sum: Plotinus’s ontological triad (Being–Life–Intellect) becomes for Pico the template of Trinitarian participation . Felicity (20.7) redefines mystical union as intellectual participation in God’s causal act. Virtue and purification (20.8–9, 20.11) translate Neoplatonic ethics into Thomist perfectionism. Non-descent of the soul (20.2) and double life (20.4–5) become the basis for Pico’s angelic anthropology in the Oratio and Heptaplus . Critique of Ficino : Pico rejects Plotinus’s ineffable One as pure negation, insisting instead on energetic causality —a God who acts rather than merely transcends. Thus, Pico’s Plotinian theses are less a repetition of the Enneads than a reconstruction of Plotinus through Aquinas and Aristotle , transforming mystical Neoplatonism into a theology of intellectual freedom—the soul’s power to rise through understanding into the causal act of divine being itself【205:15†(Medieval & Renaissance Texts & Studies 167)†L21-L33】【205:4†(Variorum Collected Studies 1063)†L12-L18】【205:9†(Critical Political Theory and Radical Practice)†L42-L46】.

Pico’s Encounter with Plotinus In the Plotinian Conclusions (I.20), Pico paraphrases Enneads V and VI through this scholastic–Arabic lens: I.20.1 “Primum intelligibile non est extra primum intellectum” — reads the Plotinian Nous through Avicenna’s identification of intellect and intelligible, collapsing their distinction in divine act. I.20.7 “Foelicitas hominis ultima est cum particularis intellectus noster totali primoque intellectui plene coniungitur” — the Arabic Plotinus turns union into participation in God’s act, not absorption; a Thomistic nuance Pico inherits. Thus Pico’s reading of Plotinus is not purely Ficinian emanationism , but one re-Aristotelized through scholastic reception of Avicenna and Averroes, where intellect is both formal and efficient cause . 3. Ficino’s Plotinus privileged emanation as necessity ; Pico, via Proclus and the Liber de Causis , reasserts divine will . Hence Pico’s “prisca theologia” is not a romantic unity but a hierarchy of causal agents analogous to the Arabic chain of intellects. In Howlett’s and Edelheit’s terms, Pico turns Neoplatonic necessity into Aristotelian act-and-potency relations illuminated by grace and intellect. 4. how does proclus relate to energeia and the aristotelian form stuff

Excellent question — this goes to the nerve center of why Pico turns from Plotinus to Proclus and why Proclus is so useful for re-framing everything (magic, angelology, act, potency) in an Aristotelian idiom without betraying Platonism. Let’s break this down carefully, from ancient metaphysics → medieval reception → Pico’s synthesis. 🜍 1. Aristotle: Energeia and the Form–Matter Relation For Aristotle, energeia (actuality, activity) is what makes a thing be in act rather than merely in potential ( dynamis ). Form is actuality ( to eidos energeia estin — “form is the act of matter,” Metaph. Θ.8). The divine first principle ( theos noesis noeseos , Metaph. Λ.9) is pure act ( actus purus ), identical with its own activity of thought. So the metaphysics of act/potency becomes a theory of how being and knowing coincide in divine self-thinking. 🜍 2. Plotinus: Energeia as Emanation — but Without Matter Plotinus takes over energeia but transposes it into pure metaphysical causation : The One overflows ( prohodos ) by its own plenitude; Nous (Intellect) is an energeia of the One — “a living act of vision.” Each level is less unified: Being → Life → Intellect → Soul . However, Plotinus rejects the Aristotelian form–matter coupling ; for him matter is privation. Energeia is divine radiance, not a form actualizing a substrate. That means Plotinus’s energeia is self-differentiating , not relational — no inner grammar of act/potency. 🜍 3. Proclus: Re-Aristotelianizing Platonism This is where Proclus steps in and becomes Pico’s secret weapon. (a) Triadic Metaphysics Proclus reinterprets energeia through triadic processions : Monê – Próodos – Epistrophê (remaining – procession – return) Each divine level remains in itself , proceeds into otherness , and returns to unity through its energeia . → This supplies a causal grammar analogous to Aristotle’s act/potency: “Remaining” = act (form retaining identity); “Procession” = the potency or capacity to communicate act; “Return” = re-actualization (energeia complete in reflection). (b) Energeia as Formal Causality Proclus explicitly defines energeia as the self-manifestation of essence ( Stoicheiosis Theologias , props. 15–20): “Every being is characterized by three things: its existence, its power ( dynamis ), and its activity ( energeia ). Essence is cause of power, power is cause of activity.” That’s an Aristotelian chain: form → potency → act . But Proclus universalizes it—each level of reality has its own act/potency relation within itself , not only between matter and form. So he integrates Aristotelian causation into Platonic procession . → Energeia is the way the One’s goodness actualizes form through the mediation of being, life, and intellect. 🜍 4. Medieval Reception: The Liber de Causis and De Ente et Essentia The Liber de Causis (an Arabicized Proclus) translates energeia as causa essendi —the “cause by which being is in act.” Aquinas re-reads this as proof that: “Omnis causa influit esse in suum effectum.” (“Every cause infuses being into its effect.”) So by Pico’s time, the Proclean chain of act and potency had become a standard metaphysical framework in scholasticism: everything acts by communicating its form ( energeia formalis ). 🜍 5. Pico’s Appropriation: Form and Energeia as Divine Communication Pico fuses this Proclean–Aristotelian framework into his own theology of magic and angels: (a) Heptaplus I.iii “The eye, that is, the intellectual substance, is not wholly simple… angels are composed of act and potency.” He’s paraphrasing Proclus’s triadic “being–life–intellect” and Aristotle’s form–matter at once: Angelic intellects are forms in act (energeiai), yet they have potency to receive further light. That is the Proclean model of “remaining–procession–return” translated into Aristotelian metaphysics. (b) Conclusiones Proclianae I.24.18–19 “Whatever divine or natural operations there are, they are double: immanent and transeunt.” “Through the immanent, each thing contains itself; through the transeunt, it converts toward externals.” This is a direct reworking of Proclus’s doctrine of immanent and proceeding activities (Elements of Theology, props. 23–25). It’s the same as saying: every form has energeia both within itself (as immanent act) and through itself (as communication of act). → That’s exactly the Aristotelian form-matter relation universalized: all beings are formal causes acting on lower potencies. 🜍 6. Why Pico Needs This 1. To replace Plotinian necessity with causal intelligibility. - In Plotinus, the One overflows by necessity. - In Proclus (and Pico), every act proceeds according to form and intention — divine will articulated as formal act . 2. To explain “legitimate magic.” - Magic = understanding how energeiai pass through forms. - That’s Proclean: “Every effect remains in its cause, proceeds from it, and returns to it.” - Knowing that chain = the philosopher’s way of participating in divine act. 3. To reconcile Aristotle and Plato under Christian providence. - Aristotelian: every act perfects a potency. - Proclean: every procession returns to its cause. - Pico’s synthesis: divine act perfects creation through intelligible forms— grace as energeia . 🜍 7. Summary Table Concept Aristotle Plotinus Proclus Pico Energeia Act of form actualizing matter Radiant activity of the One Self-manifestation of essence (immanent + transeunt) Divine illumination communicated through forms Potency Capacity for act Lower degree of being Power ( dynamis ) inherent in each level Created intellect’s openness to act Causation Efficient + formal Emanative necessity Formal communication ( per participationem ) Graceful interlocking of causes Goal of Ascent Full actualization of intellect Union with the One Reversion through act Participation in divine energeia (intellect + love) In Short Proclus is the hinge that lets Pico: Keep Plato’s hierarchy, Integrate Aristotle’s act/potency grammar, And reinterpret magic and angelology as metaphysical participation in divine energeia . Where Plotinus had made energeia a mystical outflow, Proclus—and through him Pico—makes it the intelligible operation of form itself . That’s why, for Pico, to know is to act in God’s act —an Aristotelian energeia re-enchanted by Proclean theology.

Excellent — you’ve now gotten to the heart of Pico’s metaphysical architecture , where Proclus’s Stoicheiosis Theologias (Elements of Theology) and Thomas Aquinas’s act–potency metaphysics meet in a Christian reconfiguration of Dionysius . Pico’s De ente et uno is precisely that synthesis. Let’s unpack this in three movements: 🜍 1. What Stoicheiosis Theologias Is — Proclus’s Elements of Theology Greek title: Στοιχείωσις Θεολογική (“Theological Elementary Treatise”) Latin title (since 13th century): Elementatio Theologica It’s Proclus’s systematic metaphysical manual : 211 short propositions with proofs, arranged geometrically (“Euclid for theology”). The Structure Each proposition states a principle about being, causality, procession, and return. Core examples (the ones Pico uses): Prop. Doctrine Pico’s appropriation 1–4 All things proceed from the One; each cause contains its effects in a higher mode The Heptaplus ’s “Spirit borne upon the waters”: divine act pervades creation 7–10 Every cause is superior to its effects Basis for Oratio ’s dignity of intellect and hierarchy of being 15–20 Every being has essence ( ousia ), power ( dynamis ), and activity ( energeia ) Becomes Pico’s “act and potency in angels” 23–25 Activities are double: immanent and proceeding Echoed verbatim in Conclusiones Proclianae I.24.19 30–32 All things remain, proceed, and return Monê–Próodos–Epistrophê : his model for grace, magic, and intellect 133 All reversion is through likeness Pico’s basis for imitatio Dei and “assimilation to divine act” Why it mattered Proclus gave Christian scholastics and Platonists a causal logic that could be expressed in Aristotelian terms: Energeia = actualization Dynamis = potency or power Eidos = formal cause Monê/Próodos/Epistrophê = metaphysical equivalents of act/potency/completion So Stoicheiosis Theologias provided the grammar for “metaphysical causation” —a chain of beings acting through participation. 🜍 2. The Thomistic Connection — Actus Essendi and the Liber de Causis When the Elements was translated (William of Moerbeke, c.1268), Aquinas already knew it indirectly through the Liber de Causis (an Arabic summary of Proclus). Thomas, reading it as an Aristotelian text , redefined its ideas in a Christian idiom: Proclean Principle Thomistic Adaptation Every cause contains its effects in a higher mode “Omnis causa influit esse in suum effectum.” (ST I.45.6) – every cause gives being according to its form Being, power, activity Esse – potentia – operatio → God as actus purus Immanent/transeunt acts Distinction between God’s immanent act ( intelligere ) and ad extra creation Return through likeness Creation’s final cause: rational beings return by similitudo and amor So, by Pico’s century, Proclus was already absorbed into scholastic metaphysics —especially in the idea that being = act of existence (esse) and that causality is communication of act . 🜍 3. Dionysius: From Proclean Hierarchy to Christian Theurgy Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite (late 5th c.) was himself a Christianizing Proclus . He borrowed: the triadic causal structure (remaining, procession, return); the notion of divine names as energetic powers ( energeiai ); and the principle “every effect remains in its cause.” In Dionysius: God is beyond being ( hyperousios ), but “Being” itself is his first energeia ; angels and souls participate through illumination and return by likeness ( Divine Names IV; Celestial Hierarchy ). So the Dionysian corpus already translated Proclean metaphysics into Christian theology , just as the Liber de Causis did for Aristotle. 🜍 4. Pico’s De Ente et Uno — Where it All Converges Now Pico, at 23 years old, is standing on this triple inheritance: Aristotle’s act/potency ; Proclus’s energeia -triads; Dionysius’s hierarchia of illumination; all filtered through Aquinas’s actus essendi . His goal in De Ente et Uno (1489) is to reconcile Aristotle and Plato by showing that unity (Plato) and being (Aristotle) are really identical in God , differing only conceptually. “Ens et unum convertuntur.” (“Being and the One are convertible.”) How Proclus enters 1. From Proclus: the causal axiom that every cause produces its like while remaining itself (Elem. Th. prop. 18). 2. From Aristotle: that every act perfects potency according to form. 3. From Dionysius: that divine unity “pours forth being upon all things while remaining one.” Pico fuses them: “Unity is the cause of being, as form is the cause of actuality; and as being proceeds from the One, so act proceeds from essence.” That’s Stoicheiosis Theologias rewritten in act-and-potency terms. The De Ente argument Step 1: In created things, act and potency are distinct (Aristotle). Step 2: In God, essence = act = unity (Proclus). Step 3: Participation = the analogical relation of potency to act (Dionysius). Step 4: Therefore, Aristotle’s “actus essendi” = Plato’s “One,” both describing the same divine source. He even quotes Dionysius’s Divine Names V: “The One is all things and no thing, because it is the cause of all.” Then glosses it as a Proclean triad: “remains, proceeds, returns.” 🜍 5. The Christianization of Energeia For Pico: Energeia = the mode by which divine unity gives being. Angels are energeiai in act; humans are potencies seeking act; God is actus purus . “To know” is itself a participation in divine energeia — thus magic and illumination coincide. Hence the line in Heptaplus : “God, as first act, sheds light upon intellect; intellect upon soul; soul upon nature.” That is literally Proclus’s chain, baptized and Aristotle-ized. 🜍 6. Summary Table Source Key Doctrine Pico’s Recasting Aristotle Act ( energeia ) perfects potency; God = pure act Provides logic of participation Proclus (Stoicheiosis) Every being has essence, power, and activity; triads of causation Supplies causal architecture Dionysius Divine procession & return through illumination Supplies theological hierarchy Aquinas Actus essendi = formal participation in God’s being Supplies scholastic idiom Pico, De Ente et Uno Unity and Being convertible; divine act as illumination Synthesizes all above: “God as act of unity” In Short Stoicheiosis Theologias is Proclus’s metaphysical geometry of causation . Aquinas and Dionysius already Christianized it: act = participation in divine being. Pico’s De Ente et Uno completes the circle — merging Proclean triads with Thomistic energeia and Dionysian illumination into a single doctrine: > God is the pure act of unity; all beings exist by participating in that act. That’s why De Ente is both his “anti-Ficino” and his “meta-Aquinas”: it renders Proclus’s energeia-ontology the hidden scaffolding of a Christian metaphysics of light, intellect, and freedom.

Let’s unpack this carefully, in five parts: (1) Pico’s access to Jewish sources; (2) how he reads Kabbalah metaphysically; (3) what it contributes to his angelology; (4) how it transforms his Christian Platonism; and (5) what modern scholars now say about this synthesis. 🜍 1. These texts emphasized the ten sefirot as dynamic channels ( qavod , glory, or energeiai ) through which divine life flows into creation. Mithridates glossed them for Pico using Neoplatonic and Dionysian vocabulary , describing the sefirot as “angelic powers,” “intellectual lights,” and “causes of being.” So Pico received Kabbalah not as esoterica, but as a metaphysics of illumination already harmonized with Greek theology. “There is no science which certifies us more of the divinity of Christ than Magic and Cabala.” ( Conclusiones cabalisticae , II.9.9) This claim is not hyperbole — it reflects his conviction that Kabbalah offered a technical vocabulary for describing how divine unity becomes intelligible multiplicity — precisely what Aristotle and Proclus left incomplete. 🜍 2. Kabbalah as a Metaphysics of Divine Emanation and Form In Pico’s Latin notes and Conclusiones cabalisticae , the sefirot become formal causes , energeiai , and angelic intelligences — the “living forms” by which divine act orders the universe. Hebrew Concept Pico’s Latin / Scholastic Analogue Function Ein Sof (“Infinite”) Deus absconditus / Unum super ens Transcendent unity beyond being Keter–Hokhmah–Binah Trinitarian intellectus agens et patiens Divine triad of knowing and willing Tiferet Forma Christi / Logos Mediator and perfect form of forms Netzah–Hod–Yesod Angelic causation / virtutes et ordines Channels of divine operation Malkhut Anima mundi / Materia glorificata Receptive potency of creation Thus, Kabbalah supplies a dynamic model of formal causation : Form is no static blueprint; it’s an angelic energy (like Proclus’s “intellectual gods” or Dionysius’s “celestial lights”). Angels as “Living Forms”: Kabbalah and the Chain of Being (a) The Angelic Order In the Heptaplus , Pico interprets Genesis’ “Let there be light” as the moment when divine energeia becomes multiplicity — the creation of angelic intellects. He aligns this with the Kabbalistic doctrine of ‘Orot (lights) emanating from Ein Sof . “God said, Let there be light — that is, intellects… they are minds who live by understanding, and from them flows every form into matter.” ( Heptaplus I.iii) Each angel is thus a form actualizing a potency — an Aristotelian reading of a Kabbalistic structure. (b) The Human Analogue The human intellect mirrors the angelic one: The soul in potency receives act through illumination from these forms . Hence knowledge and salvation are not mystical fusion but participation in angelic energeia . This is how Pico bridges Kabbalah and Aristotelian anthropology : the same causal light descending through the sefirot also animates the human mind — the intellect becomes a microcosmic Yesod receiving Tiferet’s illumination. 🜍 4. Kabbalah as Corrective to Ficino and Completion of Proclus Pico believed that Kabbalah resolved what Ficino and even Proclus could not: Ficino’s Plotinianism ended in pneuma (a psychological ascent through spiritus). Proclus’s system described divine causality but lacked a revealed vocabulary for its divine will and incarnation. Kabbalah gave Pico: 1. A revealed metaphysics of form. The sefirot are both ideas and wills — God’s “energies” in the world. 2. A Christological key. Tiferet (Beauty) symbolizes the Logos — the mediating form of divine unity and created plurality. 3. A natural theology of angels. The sefirot double as angelic orders — each an intellect that governs a sphere, echoing Dionysius’ nine choirs. Hence Pico writes in one cabalistic conclusion : “The angels are the forms of the divine names, which the Hebrews call Shemoth , the Greeks Ideas , and the Latins Formae intelligibiles .” This line alone demonstrates his fusion of Plato, Aristotle, and Kabbalah: angel = form = divine name = energeia. 🜍 5.
