This is a perfect question — because Giulio Busi and Raphael Ebgi’s monumental Einaudi volume Giovanni Pico della Mirandola: mito, magia, Qabbalah (2014) is the first study that actually reconstructs, line by line, how Pico used his Jewish, Arabic, and scholastic mediators to build his own metaphysical system rather than simply import foreign mysticism. The essence of Busi’s project is: Pico was not a passive receiver of Kabbalah, but a creative systematizer — an autonomous philosopher who reorganized Jewish mystical cosmology into a Christian metaphysics of angelic form and divine act. Let’s unpack what this means in relation to Pico’s engagement with sources , his method of interpretation , and the bearing on angelic forms and metaphysical causation . 🜍 1. Busi & Ebgi’s Project: What They Did Busi and Ebgi’s 600-page Mito, magia, Qabbalah reconstructs: Pico’s Kabbalistic library and Latin translations by Flavius Mithridates ; His reading notebooks ( Adnotationes super conclusiones and Commentationes cabalisticae ); The intertextual weave linking Conclusiones magicae et cabalisticae , Heptaplus , Oratio , and De ente et uno . They trace Pico’s mosaic of sources — Averroes, Avicenna, Abulafia, Gikatilla, Maimonides, Pseudo-Dionysius, Aquinas, Ficino, and Proclus — and show how Pico filtered each through a hermeneutic of assimilation , not subordination. “Pico transforms the wisdom of his mediators into a new architecture of thought: the Kabbalah becomes an interpretive method, not merely an inherited body of dogma.” — Busi & Ebgi, Mito, magia, Qabbalah , p. 38 🜍 2. Mediators and Interpreters: How Pico Read His Sources (a) Flavius Mithridates – the first mediator Busi & Ebgi reconstruct Mithridates’s Latin translations as dynamic commentaries , filled with marginal glosses equating: sefirot = “intelligentiae” or “virtutes angelicae” Ein Sof = “Deus absconditus” or “Ens omnium causa” Hokhmah / Binah = “intellectus agens / patiens” Mithridates’s glosses taught Pico to read the Kabbalah as a metaphysics of intellect , but Pico then Christianized it beyond his translator’s intention — seeing Tiferet as the Logos and Yesod as the angelic conduit of incarnation. “Mithridates provided the lexicon, but Pico turned it into metaphysics.” — Busi, p. 112 (b) Averroes and Avicenna Through scholastic summaries (and the De Causis tradition), Pico re-read Kabbalistic procession ( atzilut ) as emanation by divine act , not by necessity. This shows his interpretive autonomy : he filters Kabbalistic ideas through Aristotelian act/potency grammar. → Angels are not hypostases but forms in act . (c) Maimonides and the negative theology of the Guide Busi shows Pico engaging Maimonides as a rational counterpart to Kabbalah: negative theology becomes formal causality , where God’s unknowability = His pure act ( actus purus ). Thus, even the apophatic element of Kabbalah becomes a metaphysics of act , not mysticism. 🜍 3. Pico’s Interpretive Autonomy Busi & Ebgi argue that Pico’s method of reading his sources is midrashic rather than scholastic : He collects fragments ( seferot , names, definitions) and recombines them into new patterns. Each fragment is treated as an energeia , an act of meaning that mirrors the divine structure it describes. In other words: Pico imitates the very metaphysics he studies — he performs creation by combination . “To Pico, to interpret is to re-enact creation itself: he reproduces in thought the divine act of connecting forms.” — Ebgi, p. 229 Thus, interpretive autonomy for Pico = metaphysical imitation . He doesn’t merely cite Proclus or Gikatilla — he reenacts their causal method as hermeneutics. 🜍 4. Consequences for Angelic Metaphysics Busi and Ebgi explicitly tie this autonomy to Pico’s angelic metaphysics : (a) Angels as Forms In Mithridates’s translations, angels correspond to sefirot and to divine names . Pico reinterprets this to mean that: Each angel is a form in act , Each form is an intelligible name , Each name is an energeia or divine operation. So angelology becomes a Kabbalistic-Peripatetic hybrid : “The angels are the intelligible energies that sustain the cosmos, and the cosmos is their body.” — Busi, p. 310 (b) Kabbalah as a “Metaphysics of Forms” Pico uses Kabbalah to make visible what Aristotelian metaphysics can only say abstractly: Form = Shekhinah or Tiferet : the living presence of God in act. Potency = Malkhut : the world’s receptive intellect. Energeia = Ruach Elohim : the breath that animates the hierarchy. Hence, for Busi, Pico’s originality lies in “his decision to treat the Kabbalah as Aristotelian metaphysics written in symbolic form.” → The Hebrew letters and names are not occult; they’re metaphysical notation. (c) Eros and Ascent Busi adds that Pico’s adaptation of Kabbalistic eros (the yearning among the sefirot ) links directly to his Heptaplus angelology: “The angels ascend and descend like Jacob’s ladder: not in space but in act, perfecting their own form by desiring the higher.” — Ebgi, p. 335 This is how Pico fuses Proclean triads (remaining–procession–return) with Kabbalistic eros : every form is an act that desires its cause. 🜍 5. What Busi & Ebgi Reveal About Pico’s “Autonomy” (a) Independence from Ficino Pico’s “magus” is not Ficino’s pneumatic priest of the cosmos, but a metaphysician of act: Ficino’s spiritus mundi = psychological medium Pico’s angelus formalis = intellectual act “Pico replaces Ficino’s naturalized spirit with angelic intellect; causation becomes formal and intelligible, not pneumatic.” — Busi, p. 400 (b) Freedom through Synthesis By absorbing scholastic, Arabic, and Jewish sources into a single metaphysical syntax, Pico creates what Busi calls: “A new language of freedom — the intellect liberated to participate in divine causality.” — Busi, p. 428 Thus, Pico’s autonomy is not rebellion but metaphysical freedom : the power to read creation’s forms as God’s ongoing act — a Kabbalistic energeia of intellect. 🜍 6. In Summary Dimension Old View (Yates, Walker) Busi & Ebgi’s Revision Source use Esoteric syncretism, uncritical mixing Conscious, systematic hermeneutics Kabbalah Mystical revelation Metaphysical grammar of form and act Angels Spiritual intermediaries Intelligible forms, divine names in act Method Eclectic compilation Midrashic recombination = metaphysical creativity Goal Hidden unity of religions Theological anthropology of intellect and will Magic Occult praxis Contemplative participation in causal energeia In short The Busi project proves that Pico was not borrowing Kabbalah to ornament Christian Platonism , but using it to reform metaphysics itself . He saw in the sefirot a living model of angelic form as divine act — the energeia by which God continuously creates. His interpretive autonomy lay in translating across traditions without losing metaphysical precision : from Hebrew symbolism → to Aristotelian causality → to Proclean triads → to Christian illumination. This is why, for Busi, Pico’s Kabbalah isn’t mysticism — it’s a metaphysics of angelic forms , a new “grammar of creation” that makes the cosmos itself a network of living intelligible acts.

For Pico, this links Thomistic actus essendi — God’s self-communicating being — with a Kabbalistic metaphysics of emanation. The ascent of intellect in De ente et uno mirrors this: created forms participate in divine unity not as separate hypostases (as Ficino’s Plotinian model suggested) but as energeiai within providential order. 3. Busi & Ebgi: autonomy and symbolism Busi and Ebgi’s archival work on Pico’s Hebrew sources shows how independently he adapted them. They note Pico’s use of the Kabbalistic tsimtsum motif — “ il Dio che abbandona, Colui che s’eclissa in solitaria retractio ,” the divine self-withdrawal — as the metaphysical background for creation【226:0†Re-evaluating Pico†L24-L28】. The sefirot become “psychological attributes of the mysterious divine soul… coloring the inner life of emanation”【226:11†Re-evaluating Pico†L10-L17】. Pico mapped this onto the anima mundi and the angelic intellect: his tenfold adaptation of Dionysius’s orders aligns the hierarchy of intellects with divine self-articulation rather than a chain of being. Busi even finds Pico assigning each faculty of the soul to a sefirah : “ Io adatto la nostra anima alle dieci sefirot… la settima verso le realtà superiori, l’ottava verso le inferiori, la nona a un misto di entrambe ”【226:12†Giovanni Pico della Mirandola – Mito, magia, Qabbalah†L1-L10】. 4. From metaphysics to freedom of intellect Against Ficino’s Neoplatonic determinism, Pico re-reads the sefirot and angelic hierarchies as expressions of divine free act . In De ente et uno , as Bradshaw’s Aristotelian context helps explain, act ( energeia ) and form coincide in God’s will, which remains free and self-contained【226:10†Aristotle East and West†L1-L8】. The Kabbalistic schema allows Pico to extend this freedom analogically to human intellect: to contemplate the sefirot is to know how causes interlock under providence, and so to act “not by compulsion but by participation in divine intellect.” 5. Summary insight The fusion of Kabbalah, Thomism, and anti-Ficinian Platonism yields a new metaphysics of the angelic: The sefirot = forms of divine act ( energeiai ) mediated by intellects. Angels = living forms of divine causality, not hypostatic gods. Human intellect = potential act seeking union through contemplation ( debequt ), echoing Dionysius’s epopteia . Thus Pico’s engagement with the sefirot converts Ficino’s emanationist hierarchy into a theology of free creative intellect , where knowing the structure of creation — the “grammar of divine forms” — becomes both metaphysics and mystical ascent.
